title: Message Delivery: Guarantees, Ordering & Idempotency
description: At-most-once vs at-least-once vs "exactly-once," why duplicates and gaps happen, and how idempotency, dedup, sequence numbers, and per-key ordering tame them. The duplicate/dedup behavior and HTTP idempotency facts are verified in Node and against RFC 9110.

questions.title: In an at-least-once system, why can a receiver get the same message twice even when the network never corrupts data?
questions.description: Assume bytes are never garbled — yet duplicates still appear. What causes them?

options.text: The receiver's acknowledgment is lost, so the sender's retry timer fires and it resends a message that was already delivered.
TCP randomly reorders and duplicates application messages.
The receiver's buffer overflows and replays old messages.
It can't — duplicates only happen under at-most-once delivery.

questions.explanation:
At-least-once means the sender keeps retrying until it sees an **acknowledgment**. The duplicate comes from a lost *ack*, not a lost *message*: the message arrives and is processed, but its ack never makes it back, so the sender times out and resends — and the receiver processes the same message again.

```js
const processed = [];
const receiver = (id) => processed.push(id);
receiver("m1");         // delivered + processed; ACK then lost
receiver("m1");         // sender resent after timeout
console.log(processed); // ["m1", "m1"]  ← double side effect
```

This is *the* reason at-least-once delivery must be paired with idempotent processing or dedup. TCP doesn't duplicate application messages, and there's no buffer "replay" — the cause is the retry-on-missing-ack loop.
questions.references: [Brave New Geek: You Cannot Have Exactly-Once Delivery](https://bravenewgeek.com/you-cannot-have-exactly-once-delivery/)

questions.title: Why do engineers say "exactly-once *delivery*" is essentially unattainable — and what do real systems offer instead?
questions.description: Think about your only two options when an acknowledgment might be lost.

options.text: Over a lossy channel you must either retry (risking duplicates) or not (risking loss); systems combine at-least-once delivery with idempotent processing to get *effectively*-once.
It's already solved by TCP, which delivers every segment exactly once.
It needs specialized hardware that isn't widely deployed yet.
It works fine as long as every message carries a UUID.

questions.explanation:
When an ack can go missing, the sender faces a forced choice: resend (and maybe duplicate) or don't (and maybe lose). You cannot have *both* guaranteed-no-loss and guaranteed-no-duplicate with certainty — the practical shadow of the **Two Generals' Problem**. So "exactly-once" is achieved at the *processing* layer, not the *delivery* layer: at-least-once delivery + idempotent handling (or dedup) = **effectively-once**.

TCP gives exactly-once *segment* delivery within one connection, but that says nothing about application-level retries across reconnects or crashes (the distractor conflates the two layers). A UUID alone changes nothing unless you also store and check it — which is exactly the dedup step.

questions.title: Which statements about idempotency in message delivery are correct?

options.text: An idempotent operation has the same effect whether it's applied once or many times.
A unique idempotency key lets the receiver recognize and drop duplicate retries.
Idempotent processing turns at-least-once delivery into an effectively-once outcome.
Idempotency also guarantees that messages are never lost.

questions.title: Messages on a single WebSocket connection arrive in the order sent. What most commonly breaks that?
questions.description: The guarantee is real — but narrow. Where does it stop applying?

options.text: Spreading messages across multiple connections, consumers, or partitions — order holds *within* one stream (TCP), not *across* several.
Nothing — a WebSocket server guarantees a global order across every client connection.
Choosing binary frames instead of text frames.
Turning on `permessage-deflate` compression.

questions.explanation:
A single TCP connection delivers bytes strictly in order, so one WebSocket or SSE stream is ordered for free. The guarantee evaporates the moment you fan out: two connections, several worker consumers, or multiple partitions can interleave arbitrarily, because nothing coordinates a global order between them.

The usual fix is **per-key ordering**: route every message that must stay ordered (all events for one user or document) through the *same* partition/connection, accepting per-key rather than global order. Frame type and compression have nothing to do with ordering.

questions.title: A queue enforces strict total ordering — each message is handled only after all earlier ones. What's the inherent cost?
questions.description: What happens when one message in the middle gets stuck?

options.text: Head-of-line blocking: one slow or stuck message delays every message queued behind it.
Messages behind it are silently dropped to keep throughput up.
Acknowledgments become impossible to combine with ordering.
There is no cost — strict ordering is free.

questions.explanation:
Strict total ordering and throughput pull against each other. If message #5 can't be processed (a slow handler, a poison message, a stalled consumer), then #6, #7, … must wait — that's **head-of-line (HOL) blocking**. It's the same phenomenon that hurts HTTP/2 over TCP, where one lost packet stalls every multiplexed stream, and that HTTP/3 over QUIC was designed to avoid.

The escape hatch is to relax "total" to "per-key": independent keys flow through independent lanes/partitions, so a stuck message blocks only its own key. You trade a global order you probably didn't need for parallelism you do.

questions.title: Which classic thought experiment proves two parties can never be *certain* they agree over a lossy channel — the theoretical root of why exactly-once delivery is unattainable?
questions.description: Two words naming the problem (about coordinating an attack). The full name adds "Problem."
questions.explanation:
The **Two Generals' Problem** (also called the Two Armies Problem): two generals must agree on a time to attack, but every messenger crossing the valley might be captured. No finite exchange of acknowledgments can make *both* sides certain the other will attack — each new ack itself needs acknowledging, forever.

That impossibility is why message delivery can't be simultaneously loss-free and duplicate-free with certainty, and therefore why you choose at-most-once or at-least-once and build *effectively*-once on top with idempotency.

questions.title: A high-frequency telemetry feed tolerates an occasional dropped reading, but duplicates would corrupt aggregate counters. Best delivery guarantee?
questions.description: Weigh the cost of a lost message against the cost of a duplicate one.

options.text: At-most-once — don't retry; accept rare loss in exchange for never double-counting.
At-least-once — retry hard and rely on dedup to clean up later.
Exactly-once — the only acceptable choice for any metric.
Any of them — the guarantee makes no practical difference here.

questions.explanation:
Choose the guarantee by asking which failure is cheaper. For metrics/telemetry, one missing sample barely moves an aggregate, but a duplicate inflates counters — so **at-most-once** (fire-and-forget, no retries) is the standard fit. This is exactly why StatsD ships metrics over **UDP**: it deliberately accepts loss to avoid retries, duplication, and backpressure on the application.

For commands, payments, or state changes the calculus flips — losing one is unacceptable, so you use at-least-once plus idempotency. The lesson: there is no universally "best" guarantee, only the right trade-off between the cost of loss and the cost of duplication.
questions.references: [Etsy Code as Craft: Measure Anything, Measure Everything](https://www.etsy.com/codeascraft/measure-anything-measure-everything/)

questions.title: You dedup by remembering the ids you've already processed. What's the practical catch?
questions.description: Consider how much history a real receiver can actually keep.

options.text: You can't remember every id forever — dedup works only within a bounded window, so a duplicate arriving after the window can still slip through.
You must store every id permanently, after which dedup is always perfect.
Deduping silently reorders the surviving messages.
Dedup only works on text frames, never binary.

questions.explanation:
Remembering processed ids costs memory, so real systems keep a **bounded dedup window** — by time (ids seen in the last 24h) or by count. A retry that arrives *after* its id has aged out of the window looks brand-new and gets processed again. That's why dedup is a best-effort *optimization*, while truly **idempotent operations** — where reprocessing is harmless by construction — are the robust foundation.

Stripe, for example, retains idempotency keys for 24 hours; Amazon's guidance is the same: pair a bounded key store with operations that are safe to repeat. Frame type is irrelevant to dedup.
questions.references: [Amazon Builders' Library: Making retries safe with idempotent APIs](https://aws.amazon.com/builders-library/making-retries-safe-with-idempotent-APIs/)

questions.title: Which techniques help a consumer preserve or restore order in a real-time stream?
questions.description: Select every technique that actually helps with ordering.

options.text: Tag each message with a monotonically increasing sequence number so the consumer can detect gaps and reorder.
Partition by a key so all messages for that key travel through one ordered lane.
Buffer briefly to reassemble out-of-order arrivals before delivering them upward.
Send each message on a brand-new connection to maximize parallelism.

questions.explanation:
Three real tools: **sequence numbers** let a receiver notice "I have 5 and 7 but not 6" and wait or request a resend; **per-key partitioning** (Kafka's model) keeps everything for one key on a single ordered log; and a small **reorder buffer** smooths out arrivals that race each other.

The false option does the opposite of all three: spraying messages across fresh connections removes the single-stream ordering guarantee entirely and maximizes interleaving. Parallelism and strict ordering pull in opposite directions — partition by key to get *both* (order within a key, parallelism across keys).

questions.title: When a system advertises "exactly-once semantics" (e.g. Kafka EOS), what does it really mean?
questions.description: Does it repeal the impossibility results — or work around them?

options.text: Inside its own boundary it combines idempotent producers with transactional commits so each record affects state once; it's not magic across arbitrary external systems.
It physically transmits each network packet exactly one time.
It disables retries so a message is never sent twice.
It is a proof that the Two Generals' Problem was wrong.

questions.explanation:
Kafka's exactly-once semantics is **effectively-once processing**, built from two ingredients: an **idempotent producer** (each message carries a producer id + sequence number, so the broker drops retries) and **transactions** (the write and the consumer-offset commit are committed atomically). Under the hood it's still at-least-once delivery plus dedup — fully consistent with the impossibility results, not a refutation of them.

The catch every senior engineer remembers: EOS holds *within* Kafka. The moment a consumer triggers an external side effect (charge a card, send an email), you're back to needing idempotency at that boundary.
questions.references: [Confluent: Exactly-Once Semantics Are Possible — Here's How Kafka Does It](https://www.confluent.io/blog/exactly-once-semantics-are-possible-heres-how-apache-kafka-does-it/)

questions.title: Per RFC 9110, how many HTTP methods are idempotent (the safe methods plus PUT and DELETE)?
questions.description: Count them: the safe methods, plus PUT and DELETE. Give a single number.
questions.explanation:
RFC 9110 defines **6** idempotent methods: the four *safe* ones — `GET`, `HEAD`, `OPTIONS`, `TRACE` — plus `PUT` and `DELETE`. Repeating any of them has the same server-visible effect as a single call, so a client can safely retry them after a timeout.

`POST` and `PATCH` are **not** idempotent — retrying a `POST` may create a second resource or double-charge a card. That's precisely why payment APIs require an `Idempotency-Key` header on `POST`: it manufactures idempotency for a method that lacks it.

```js
const idempotent = ["GET", "HEAD", "OPTIONS", "TRACE", "PUT", "DELETE"];
console.log(idempotent.length);           // 6
console.log(idempotent.includes("POST")); // false
```
